pages.text: Oil palm shell as a lightweight aggregate for production high strength lightweight concrete Payam Shaﬁgh ⇑, Mohd Zamin Jumaat, Hilmi Mahmud Department of Civil Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Malaya, 50603 Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia article info Article history: Received 4 June 2010 Received in revised form 5 October 2010 Accepted 13 November 2010 Available online 18 December 2010 Keywords: High strength lightweight concrete Compressive strength Curing Agricultural solid waste Oil palm shell abstract In Malaysia, oil palm shell (OPS) is an agricultural solid waste originating from the palm oil industry. In this investigation old OPS was used for production of high strength lightweight concrete (HSLC). The den- sity, air content, workability, cube compressive strength and water absorption were measured. The effect of ﬁve types of curing conditions on 28-day compressive strength was studied. The test results showed that by incorporating limestone powder and without it, it is possible to produce the OPS concretes with 28-day compressive strength of about 43–48 MPa and dry density of about 1870–1990 kg/m 3. The com- pressive strength of OPS HSLC is sensitive to the lack of curing. The water absorption of these concretes is in the range of good concretes. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Ordinary concrete, made with natural aggregate, has a low strength–weight ratio compared to steel. Therefore, in using it there is an economic disadvantage when designing structural members for tall buildings, long-span bridges, and ﬂoating struc- tures. One of the ways to resolve this problem is through using high strength lightweight aggregate concrete [1]. High Strength Low Density Concrete (HSLDC) typically achieves strength levels from 34 to 69 MPa. This concrete has an air dry density not exceed- ing 2000 kg/m 3 as determined by ASTM C 567 [2]. HSLC with com- pressive strength targets ranging from 35 to 41 MPa has been successfully used for precast and prestressed concrete producers in North America. In addition, tests with ultimate strengths approaching 69 MPa are ongoing [3]. By using a high cement con- tent and good quality lightweight aggregate of small size i.e. 9 or 13 mm maximum it has been possible in some precast and pre- stressing plants to produce 40–50 MPa concrete [1]. The most popular method for lightweight concrete production is to use lightweight aggregate (LWA) [4] and, in most cases, the lightweight concrete has been made using a lightweight coarse aggregate and a normal weight sand for the ﬁne aggregate [5]. Expanded clay and shale are two artiﬁcial lightweight aggregates that are most suitable for the production of structural quality lightweight concrete [1]. These types of commercially available lightweight aggregates are obtained through heat treatment at 1000–1200 /C176C [6]. This temperature results in high fuel costs. Therefore, it would be beneﬁcial to ﬁnd a lightweight aggregate from a natural source. But with the increasing use of these types of lightweight aggregates consequently, it will cause a severe depletion of the natural resources. An alternative source for light- weight aggregate is the utilization of lightweight waste materials. Using these types of lightweight aggregates will not only provide a signiﬁcant saving in the overall cost of construction but also, by reducing solid waste it will address the concomitant environmen- tal problems. Oil palm shell (OPS) or palm kerne

to determine the inﬂuence of curing conditions on the compressive strength of high strength OPS concrete. 2. Experiment programme 2.1. Materials 2.1.1. Cement and ﬁller The cement used was Malaysia ordinary Portland cement (OPC), having a 3-, 7- and 28-day compressive strength of 25.5, 34.2 and 45.9 MPa, respectively. The spe- ciﬁc gravity of the cement used was 3.14 g/cm 3. Its Blaine speciﬁc surface area was 3510 cm2/g. Its chemical composition is given in Table 1 . Fine limestone powder, with 92% <75 lm, 85% <45 lm and Blaine speciﬁc surface area of 7270 cm 2/g, was used as ﬁller and as a part of the ﬁne aggregate. The chemical compositions of the cement and limestone powder used, expressed in percentages by mass of the constituent oxides, are shown in Table 1 . 2.1.2. Aggregates Local mining sand with a ﬁneness modulus of 2.73, speciﬁc gravity of 2.68, water absorption of 0.96% was used as the ﬁne aggregate. OPS were used as the coarse aggregate. They were collected from a local crude palm oil producing mill, and comprised old discarded waste at the palm oil mill area. Old OPS means that they are discarded for more than 6 months at the palm oil mill area. The difference between old and fresh OPS is in the ﬁbre content on the surface. Usually more than 50% of fresh OPS grains have ﬁbre. This percentage for old OPS is less than 5%. Fig. 1 shows the OPS grains with and without ﬁbre. These ﬁbres increase the demand for water and also diminish the contact area between the mortar and OPS surface that result in a weak interfacial transition zone. Conse- quently, to achieve better workability of fresh OPS concrete and the bond between OPS and mortar phase, the old OPS were used. Before the OPS were used, they were washed and sieved. They were then soaked in water for 24 h, and subsequently air dried in the laboratory to obtain approximately a saturated surface dry condition. For investigation of the size effect of OPS aggregate on compressive strength, OPS aggregate passing through the 12.5 and 9.5 mm sieve were used to obtain two types of OPS aggregate with a difference in maximum size. The physical properties and grading of OPS are shown in Tables 2 and 3 , respectively. 2.1.3. Superplasticizer Trial mixes show that the selection of the type of superplasticizer (SP) and its dosage are very important to improve the workability of fresh OPS concrete. The importance of using superplasticizer in oil palm shell concrete was reported by Okafor [17]. Hence, several types of SP were tested. The SP used in this study was Sika ViscoCrete-15RM, supplied from Sika, is in conformity with EN 934-2, and was used as 1%, 1.5% and 1.8% of cement weight. 2.1.4. Water The water used in all mixes was potable water from the pipeline in the lab. 2.2. Mix proportions Eight different OPS concrete mixtures were produced, as shown in Table 4. The cement content was 480, 520 and 550 kg/m 3. The effect of maximum coarse aggre- gate size was compared with mix 1 (12.5 mm maximum size) and mix 2. The max- imum OPS size for all mixes, except mix 1 was 9.5 mm. For investigation of the effect of limestone powder as a ﬁller material on the fresh and hardened OPS concrete, three additional mixes were made to compare the effect of partial replacement limestone powder (10, 20 and 30% of OPC by weight) by the ﬁne aggregate (mixes 3–5). In addition, the effect of increasing the OPS amount on the properties of high strength OPS concrete (mixes 7 and 8) were compared. 2.3. Curing

For determination of water absorption of all mixes at the age of 28-day, speci- mens were dried in the oven at 100 ± 5 /C176C to reach a constant mass and then fully immersed in water with 20 ± 2 /C176C for 72 h. This test was similar to the test con- ducted by Razak et al. [18] and Teo et al. [19]. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Properties of fresh concrete Table 5 summarizes the results for the slump values of fresh concrete samples that were tested immediately after mixing and also at times of 15, 30 and 45 min to determine the slump loss of the mixtures. It can be seen that, all the concretes made (except mix 5) had a maximum slump at 15 min or 30 min. This phenom- enon shows that to achieve the better workability for OPS concrete it needs more time for mixing. In general, all mixes exhibit a good to high workability. No segregation and bleeding occurred in any of the mixes produced. By improving the workability and compatibil- ity of the mixture, the strength of the interfacial transition zone tends to improve and, thus, improves the strength of the concrete. The effect of the maximum size of OPS on workability was dem- onstrated by mixes 1 and 2. With the same proportions, the con- crete slump increased slightly when the maximum size of OPS aggregate was changed from 9.5 mm to 12.5 mm. The effect of limestone powder on workability was demon- strated by mixes 2–5. Adding limestone powder resulted in the reduction of workability of the concrete. Especially for mix 5, from the time of leaving the mixer, the concrete presented a signiﬁ- cantly slump loss. The air content of concrete mixes 1 and 2 was 2.2% and 1.6%, respectively. Teo et al. [19] reported that the air content in the OPS concrete is in the range of 4.8–5.5%. They attributed this high air content to the highly irregular shapes of the OPS which pre- vented full compaction from being achieved, as well as the high water absorption of OPS. From the results of this study (decrease air content more than 50%), it seems that the ﬁrst reason is the most effective for the high air content. This is because, in OPS aggregates, when the maximum size is reduced (from 12.5 mm to 9.5 mm in this study) the variety of shapes diminish. The average air content of the concrete with limestone powder was 1.4%. A decrease in the air content causes an increase in the compressive strength. Al-Khaiat and Haque [20] reported an aver- age air content of 1.7% for high strength lightweight concrete (HSLC), which was produced using Lytag coarse and ﬁne aggregate. In addition, for Brazilian (expanded clay) high performance light- weight concrete, an average air content of 2.7% was reported [21]. 3.2. Compressive strength and density Fresh unit and oven dry weights for all the concrete mixtures are given in Table 6 with the standard variations. The dry concrete density varied from 1868 to 1988 kg/m 3. If the weight of ordinary concrete in 2300 kg/m 3 to be considered there is a saving in the dead load between about 14% and 19%. The compressive strength of the full water cured concretes is in- cluded in Table 7. The 28-day compressive strength of the OPS con- cretes varies between around 43 and 48 MPa, which suggests that these concretes are high strength. The pore structure, density, and strength may vary within a wide range for different types of light- weight aggregate. Hence, not all types of lightweight aggregate are suitable for production of high-strength concrete [22]. This inves- tigation shows th

maximum size of OPS aggregate signiﬁcantly improves the 56-day strength of concrete. The highest 1 day compressive strength with an average of about 30 MPa was obtained for all mixes containing limestone powder (mixes 3–5), because of the ﬁller effect. Therefore, this concrete, with an average dry density of about 1900 kg/m 3 can be used for precast concrete manufacturing. The optimum percentage limestone ﬁller (by weight of cement, replaced by ﬁne aggregate) of 20% and the highest value of com- pressive strength of 48.33 MPa were obtained in this study. Mix 6 is a high strength OPS concrete with a cement content of 480 kg/m3. This quantity of cement is with the usual range of val- ues used in the mix design of lightweight concrete [8]. The 28-day compressive strength of this mix was about 47 MPa. This result shows that with the usual range of cement content and the highest density that is valid for structural lightweight concrete, it is possi- ble to make OPS concrete with high strength. As can be seen from Table 6, there is not remarkable difference between mix 6 and mix 4 pertaining to 28-day compressive strength. It was reported [24,25] that the compressive strength and density of OPS concrete increase by increasing the sand to cement ratio. Mix 6 has higher sand to cement ratio and lower water to cement ratio than mix 4. But mix 4 is more interesting than mix 6 because of its lower density, better workability, lower SP content and then better com- pressive strength. A comparison between the development of compressive strength for 28-day mixes 7 and 8 shows that by increasing the quantity of OPS in mix 7 until about 15% the 28-day compressive strength and density decrease about 8% and 4.5%, respectively. This comparison shows that in OPS concrete, without using cementi- tious additions in the mixture, the lower the density, and the lower is the strength. The correlation between the density of oven dry concrete and the 28-day compressive strength of high strength OPS concrete for mixes without limestone powder (mixes 1, 2, 6–8) is presented in Fig. 3 . A suitable linear relation is developed between the den- sity and compressive strength for grades of OPS concrete ranging from 42.8 to 48.3 MPa. It is clearly seen from Fig. 2 that the com- pressive strength linearly increases with the increase of density. A relation has been proposed, which is suitable for OPS high- strength concrete. The proposed equation is given by: y = 0.04x /C029.37, where, y is the compressive strength (MPa), and x is the oven dry density (kg/m 3). This equation has an R2 of 0.96, indicating a strong relationship. Fig. 3 also shows a compari- son between the OPS LWC in this study and LWC based on ex- panded clay aggregates reported by Chen and Liu [26], and Rossignolo and Agnesini [27]. It is clear from Fig. 3 that according to the results of this study, OPS high strength lightweight concrete is available in the range of higher density. The density of the OPS high strength lightweight concrete is signiﬁcantly higher than that of manufactured lightweight aggregate (expanded clay). Proposed prediction models for compressive strength are of great concern in structural engineering. However, for high- strength concrete it is difﬁcult to predict the 28-day strengths from early strength because of the inﬂuence of the aggregate [22]. In this study, it was observed that for mixes without limestone powder there is an acceptable linear relationship between early age streng

seems that for this reason most codes of practice recommend suf- ﬁcient curing to achieve a maturity equivalent to 7 days moist cur- ing [29]. The results of this investigation show that the strength of the OPS concrete appears to be sensitive to poor curing. The sensi- tivity increasing in the concrete mixtures with high cement con- tent and low water cement ratio. It must be noted that while the compressive strength of all specimens in the lab are sensitive to curing, the same has not been shown true in larger elements [30]. 3.4. Water absorption Most good concretes have absorption below 10% by mass [31]. The water absorption of all mixes is given in Fig. 6 . In this study, it was observed that high strength OPS concretes have the lowest water absorption of 3.12% and the highest of 6.20%. For OPS con- cretes with conventional compressive strength, water absorption of more than 10% has been reported [19]. For other types of struc- tural lightweight concrete, the reports are very different. For exam- ple, a water absorption value of about 3–6% for expanded Polystyrene aggregates concrete [32] and 14–22% for Pumice aggregates [33] have been reported. 4. Conclusions Based on the experimental results of this investigation, it can be concluded that: 1. A lightweight aggregate concrete with both high workability and strength can be reached by using old OPS (OPS without ﬁbre). 2. It is possible to manufacture OPS high strength lightweight concrete with 28-day compressive strength of about 43 and 48 MPa and dry density of about 1870 and 1990 kg/m 3. 3. Compressive strength at 1, 3 and 7 day were 40–66%, 72–81% and 86–91% of the 28-day strength in OPS high-strength con- crete, respectively. 4. High strength OPS concretes are sensitive to poor curing. A minimum period of 7 days of moist curing is recommended for this type of LWAC. With this period of moist curing the compressive strength will be very similar to that of continu- ous curing. 5. Water absorption of OPS high-strength concrete varies from 3.1% to 6.2%, which is in the range of good concretes. References [1] Mehta PK, Monteiro PJM.
